intro.uz: Kayzen — har kuni kichik qadamlar bilan katta oʻzgarishga erishish. Bu haftaning ikki grammatik mavzusi: oʻtmishdan hozirgacha davom etadigan ish (present perfect — since / How long) va bosqichma-bosqich oʻzgarish (better and better). Jan haqidagi POV hikoyada ikkalasini ham eshitasiz.
intro.en: Kaizen — reaching big change through tiny daily steps. This week's two grammar topics: actions that run from the past up to now (present perfect — since / How long) and gradual change (better and better). You'll hear both in the POV story about Jan.

canDo.uz: Dars oxirida “How long have you …?” savoliga toʻxtamasdan javob bera olasiz va bosqichma-bosqich oʻzgarishni (“better and better”) tasvirlab bera olasiz.
canDo.en: By the end you can answer “How long have you …?” out loud without pausing, and describe a gradual change (“better and better”).

grammar.titleUz: Present perfect bilan tanishuv: since / for / How long
grammar.bandLifter: IELTS Part 1 — talk about how long you have done something
grammar.cefrCanDo: B1: I can say how long I have been doing something (for / since)
grammar.bodyHtml:
Bu darsning POV hikoyasida bir jumla qayta-qayta yangraydi: "Since she was a child Jan has been rude. How long has Jan been rude?" Bu — present perfect (hozirgi tugallangan zamon). Uni yodlamaymiz; POV va mini-hikoyani tinglab, quloq bilan sezasiz. Bu yerda faqat bitta narsani ochamiz — bu zamon nima maʼno beradi va nega u kerak.

Maʼnosi: oʻtmishda boshlanib, hozirgacha davom etayotgan ish

Present perfect — oʻtmish bilan hozirni bogʻlaydigan koʻprik. Ish oʻtmishning bir nuqtasida boshlangan va hozir ham davom etmoqda (yoki natijasi hozir ham seziladi). Aynan Kayzenning gʻoyasi: kichik ish oʻtmishda boshlanadi, kunlar oʻtadi, natija bugungacha yigʻilib boradi.

  • Jan has been rude since she was a child. — Jan bolaligidan beri qoʻpol (hozir ham).
  • I have studied English for three years. — Men uch yildan beri ingliz tili oʻrganaman (hali ham davom etmoqda).

Shakli sodda: have / has + V3 — feʼlning uchinchi shakli (been, studied, lived, worked, known). I / you / we / they bilan have, he / she / it bilan has. Nutqda koʻpincha qisqaradi: I've, she's, they've.

Ikki kalit soʻz — since va for (ularni adashtirmang)

Ikkalasi ham present perfect bilan tez-tez keladi, ammo butunlay boshqa narsani bildiradi:

  • since + boshlanish nuqtasi (qachondan beri): since 2020, since Monday, since she was a child. — ish qachon boshlanganini aytadi.
  • for + davomiylik (qancha vaqt): for three years, for two hours, for a long time. — ish qancha davom etganini aytadi.

Savolda How long…? ishlatiladi — "qachondan beri / qancha vaqt":

  • How long has Jan been rude? — Since she was a child. / For many years.
  • How long have you studied English? — For three years. / Since 2021.

Nega bu B1 belgisi va IELTSga foydali

Suhbatda "Bu ish bilan qachondan beri shugʻullanasiz?" degan savol doim uchraydi. "I have lived here for five years" yoki "I have known her since school" deb bemalol javob bersangiz, darajangiz koʻrinadi. Kichik farq — katta taassurot.

Eslatma: grammatikani mukammal bilishga emas, sezishga harakat qiling. POVni tinglayotganda has been, since, for soʻzlarini shunchaki payqang. Bir necha marta eshitgach, ular quloqqa tabiiy urila boshlaydi — grammatika esa faqat hikoyani tushunish uchun xizmat qiladi.
grammar.contrastUz: Oʻzbek tilida buni hozirgi zamonda aytamiz: “2020-yildan beri oʻqiyman”. Ingliz tili esa oʻtmishda boshlanib hozirgacha davom etayotgan ish uchun maxsus present perfect ishlatadi: “I have studied since 2020”. “beri” = since (boshlanish nuqtasi), “… yildan/davomida” = for (davomiylik).
grammar.errorFixUz: ❌ I study English since 2020. ✅ I have studied English since 2020. Oʻzbek oʻquvchilar since/for bilan hozirgi oddiy zamonni ishlatib yuboradi. since yoki for kelsa — present perfect (have/has + V3).

examples.en: Jan has been rude since she was a child.
I have studied English for three years.
examples.uz: Men uch yildan beri ingliz tili oʻrganaman.

examples.en: How long have you lived here?
examples.uz: Bu yerda qancha vaqtdan beri yashaysiz?

exercises.prompt: Jan ___ rude since she was a child.
exercises.hintUz: since + oʻtmish nuqtasi → present perfect (has + been)

exercises.prompt: I have studied English ___ three years.
exercises.hintUz: davomiylik (qancha vaqt) → for; “since” esa boshlanish nuqtasi uchun
How long + present perfect → have/has + V3

exercises.promptUz: Oʻzingiz haqingizda rost gap ayting (ovoz chiqarib): “I have … for/since …”.

grammar.titleUz: Bosqichma-bosqich oʻzgarish: better and better; get + comparative
grammar.bandLifter: IELTS Part 2 — describe how something changed over time
grammar.cefrCanDo: B1→B2: I can describe a gradual trend (better and better)
grammar.bodyHtml:
Kayzen — bu bosqichma-bosqich oʻsish: har kuni bir oz yaxshiroq. Ingliz tili buni ikki chiroyli qolip bilan ifodalaydi, va ikkalasini ham shu darsda eshitasiz. MAIN talkda AJ "better and better answers" deydi, POVda esa Jan ikki yil davomida "very, very kind" boʻlib boradi.

1-qolip: get / become + qiyosiy daraja (oʻzgarishni koʻrsatadi)

get yoki become feʼli "holat oʻzgaryapti" degan maʼnoni beradi. Undan keyin sifatning qiyosiy (comparative) shakli keladi:

  • My English is getting better. — Inglizcham yaxshilanyapti.
  • The questions become easier. — Savollar osonlashib boradi.
  • It is getting harder to stop. — Toʻxtash qiyinlashib boradi.

Qiyosiy shaklni yasash: qisqa sifatga -er (old → older, easy → easier), uzun sifatga more (difficult → more difficult). Notoʻgʻri shakllarni yodda tuting: good → better, bad → worse.

2-qolip: qiyosiy and qiyosiy (tobora kuchayib borishni koʻrsatadi)

Bir xil qiyosiy soʻzni and bilan takrorlasangiz, "borgan sari koʻproq" degan maʼno chiqadi — jarayon toʻxtovsiz kuchayadi:

  • better and better — tobora yaxshiroq
  • nicer and nicer — tobora yoqimliroq
  • more and more difficult — borgan sari qiyinroq (uzun sifat: more and more + sifat)

Kayzenning butun mohiyati shu jumlada: "If you practise every day, your English gets better and better."

Ikkalasini birga

  • The more you listen, the more natural it gets. — Qancha koʻp tinglasangiz, shuncha tabiiy boʻlib boradi.
  • Little by little, Jan became kinder and kinder. — Sekin-asta Jan tobora mehribonroq boʻldi.

Nega bu darajani koʻtaradi

IELTS Part 2 da "biror narsa vaqt oʻtishi bilan qanday oʻzgargani"ni tasvirlash kerak boʻladi. "My speaking got more and more confident" kabi jumla — bosqichma-bosqich oʻzgarishni aniq va ravon koʻrsatadigan B1→B2 belgisidir.

Eslatma: bu qoliplarni mashq qilishning eng yaxshi yoʻli — oʻzingiz haqingizda aytish. Har kuni bir jumla: "My English is getting better and better." Uni ovoz chiqarib ayting — miya oʻzi eslab qoladi.
grammar.contrastUz: Oʻzbekcha “tobora yaxshiroq” yoki “borgan sari yaxshiroq” bir soʻz bilan tugaydi. Ingliz tili sifatni takrorlaydi: “better and better”. “get/become” esa oʻzbekcha “-lashib bormoq” (yaxshilanib bormoq) maʼnosini beradi.
grammar.errorFixUz: ❌ more better / gooder and gooder. ✅ better and better. “good”ning qiyosiy shakli — “better” (more ham, -er ham qoʻshilmaydi). Uzun sifatlarda esa: “more and more difficult”.

examples.en: My English is getting better and better.
Little by little, she became kinder and kinder.
The more you practise, the easier it gets.
examples.uz: Qancha koʻp mashq qilsangiz, shuncha osonlashadi.

exercises.prompt: With kaizen, my English is getting ___.
exercises.hintUz: good → better; “better and better” = tobora yaxshiroq

exercises.prompt: Small daily steps make you ___ over time.
exercises.hintUz: qisqa sifat + -er, takrorlanadi: stronger and stronger

exercises.promptUz: Oʻzingiz haqingizda rost gap ayting: “My … is getting … and … .”

main: Hi, this is AJ with your next lesson. The rest of the lessons are going to be a little bit different than what you’ve had up ‘til now.

In the rest of these lessons, I’m going to talk about some book or article or idea, I’ll probably read a little bit of it just so you can get the general idea of the topic, and then I’ll talk about that topic.

And, of course, we’ll have our same mini-stories, we’re going to have our vocabulary lessons, and we’re going to add one more kind of lesson, the point of view lesson. The point of view lesson is really great for learning grammar in an intuitive way. Alright, so let’s get started. This lesson is called “The Kaizen Way.”

So we’re going to talk about a book. The book is called The Kaizen Way by Robert Maurer. Now, kaizen is a Japanese word although we use it in English now a little bit. Especially in Business you find it a lot, kaizen. Kaizen, as our Japanese students already know, means small improvements over time.

It really means constant and neverending improvement. But it has this idea of making little tiny improvements again and again and again and again, for one week, for one month, for one year, forever. So kaizen is kind of the opposite of innovation. Innovation means a sudden, big change, a sudden, big improvement.

And that’s a great way to learn and improve. For example, you could study English very intensely for one month or two months and you would make a big, sudden improvement. That’s the innovation idea, but there’s another way you can improve and that is the kaizen way.

And the kaizen way means maybe you just listen to English or read English or study English, maybe you just do it 20 minutes every day. And every day you improve just a little bit. But the key, the secret is you do it every single day. Every day you make one little improvement.

Well after one week, one month, one year, two years, five years, you will make a huge change just by making little tiny improvements over time. That’s the idea of kaizen. So I’m going to read a little bit from The Kaizen Way, just a couple paragraphs, and then I’ll talk about it more.

Okay, so here’s a section, it’s called Kaizen Tip and this is from Robert Maurer, M-a-u-r-e-r, that’s his last name. Okay, Kaizen Tip: “You want to do something creative. Write a story or a song. Paint a picture. Dream up your perfect career. Learn something or come up with a zinger of a solution to an office problem.

But you have no idea where to start. Your mind keeps coming up empty. During times like these, kaizen can help you summon your powers of inspiration. Although you can’t force your brain to cough up creative ideas on demand, you can program to launch the imaginative process simply by asking yourself a small question.

Here are some of the most popular small questions my clients use for creativity. Feel free to come up with your own. Whatever question you use, your challenge is to ask it with a gentle and patient spirit. When you use harsh or urgent tones with yourself, fear will clog the creative process.

So here are some questions you can ask yourself. Number 1, what’s one thing I wish to contribute to the world with my project or idea? Number 2, whom could I ask for help or inspiration? Number 3, what is special about my creative process, about my talents, about my team? Number 4, what type of work would excite and fulfill me?

Number 5, what small tiny change could I make now, today, to improve? Remember, if you repeat the question for several days or weeks, or however long it takes, the hippocampus, which is the part of the brain that stores information, will have no choice but to address it.

And in its own way, on its own timetable, the brain will begin giving you answers.” Okay, that’s the section from The Kaizen Way, so this is very interesting from Robert Maurer here. This technique of asking small questions to yourself is very powerful. You can use it for anything. You can use it for learning English, of course.

You could ask yourself small questions every day. For example, how could I improve today just a little bit? Or how could I improve my pronunciation 2% this week? The important part of asking these questions is that they must be small questions. So don’t say how could I be totally perfect with English in one month?

That’s a huge question and often you’ll feel stress or worry or fear if you ask this gigantic, huge question. You’ll think, oh my god, I don’t know, can I do it? But if you ask a very small question, it seems so small, so easy, your brain will say “Oh, I can do that, that’s easy.” And then you will start to take action.

And, of course, action is the most important part. Action is what we need. So if you try to ask big questions, you’re trying to improve very fast and you’re feeling stress, maybe you can change your strategy. Try the Kaizen way. Instead, ask yourself little small questions. How could I improve just a little bit?

How could I learn just one new word each day? How could I improve my pronunciation just a tiny bit each week? Ask yourself these little questions. And another point from this article, you need to repeat the questions again and again and again.

You have to ask this same question or questions every day for one week or two weeks or maybe even a couple months. By asking yourself these questions again, again, every day you’re asking the same question, your brain must come up with an answer. Your brain must find an answer. It will find answers.

Keep asking questions, your brain will give you answers. It will give you better and better answers the more you ask these small questions. So anytime you have some big goal, some big project that seems so difficult, try the kaizen way. Try to approach this problem with little small tiny

improvements. Ask yourself little small easy questions every day, again and again and again. Your brain will get more energy. It will find the answers. You’ll get momentum. You’ll start to take action. And then after one month you’ll take bigger actions. After two to three months, bigger actions, bigger actions.

Actually these improvements start to grow. The improvements get bigger and faster and faster. That’s the magic of the kaizen way. It seems so small and tiny. It seems so easy but over time it builds. It grows stronger and stronger. So try this. Try this method. Try the kaizen way.

And again, the book title is The Kaizen Way, that’s K-a-i-z-e-n, kaizen, The Kaizen Way by Robert Maurer. Alright, and remember, of course, keep your psychology strong. Keep your physiology strong. I hope you’re smiling right now. I hope those shoulders are back. Chin up. Lots of energy. Don’t forget that every single lesson.
You must be smiling. You must be moving. You must have energy in your body. That’s how you’re going to learn English much, much faster. Okay, I will see you next time.
vocab: Welcome to the vocabulary lesson for “The Kaizen Way.” Are you smiling? Big smile, remember. Body up, shoulders back. Big smile, make it bigger, come on, a huge grin. Look stupid. Look silly. What about your body? Start moving it.

If you’re sitting in a car, if you’re sitting in a train, you know you can just move your legs around a little bit. People look at you and think you’re strange, it’s okay. Hopefully you have a chance, you’re out walking. Get that iPod in your ears. Move your body. Energy, get some energy in your body. We’re going to learn some English.

Are you ready? Let’s go. Vocabulary for “The Kaizen Way.” Our first word from this article from this lesson is zinger, a zinger. Now a zinger is a surprise or a shock. So in the article, it came from the phrase “You want to come up with a zinger of a solution to an office problem.”

So a zinger of a solution means a surprise or a shock of a solution. It means a solution that is surprising, in a good way here, of course, it can be negative. Zingers can sometimes be a little bit negative, but it has this idea of something that is surprising or something that is shocking. Something that is sudden, a zinger.

Alright, our next word after zinger, our next word is summon. To summon or to summon up, sometimes we’ll say summon up, sometimes we just say summon. And in the article we had the phrase “Kaizen can help you summon your powers of inspiration.” Summon your power, to summon means to call, call something to you, right?

So to summon your power means to call your power “Power come to me now!” That’s the idea. So again, to summon means to call someone or something to you. It’s kind of like saying “Come to me. Come now.” To summon, to summon.

Next we have the phrase to cough up, to cough up. To cough up means to give or to produce quickly, right? To make something happen quickly, to give quickly. Imagine you have an idea deep in your brain and then, bleah, you force it to come out. That’s this idea of coughing up — forcing something to come out of you.

Alright, next we have the word harsh, harsh. Harsh means very rough, too strong, very bitter. It’s really the opposite of gentle. So if you use a harsh tone, it’s like “Get me an idea now!” The opposite would be gentle: “Give me an idea now please.” Harsh, arrr, very rough.

And in that same sentence we have the word clog, to clog. To clog means to block or to hinder, right? You stop something from happening. So fear will clog the creative process. It means fear will block it. If you’re too harsh with yourself, the creativity is blocked. It’s clogged.
Next we have the word fulfill. Fulfill means to satisfy, right? To fulfill means make you feel good, make you feel happy, make you feel satisfied. If you fulfill yourself, then you satisfy yourself.

Our next phrase is over the course of. We had the sentence “If you repeat the question over the course of several days or weeks, you will find an answer.” Over the course of just means for or during — a length of time. Over the course of several days means during the time of several days.

Alright, next is the word hippocampus. It’s a part of the brain that stores information. It’s kind of like your memory — like a hard disc in a computer that stores information long-term. Right now you’re putting vocabulary into that part of your brain.

Finally, we have the verb to address, to address something. Here to address means to concentrate on, to focus on, to think about, to do something about. So your brain must address the problem — it must focus on the problem, concentrate on the problem.

Now that is all of the vocabulary for “The Kaizen Way.” Listen to it several times and then go and listen to the mini-story. As always, you better be smiling! Strong physiology is so important with the Effortless English system. Use it every single time you listen to English. Alright, that is the end of “The Kaizen Way” vocabulary lesson.
pov: Hi this is AJ again and now it’s time for our new kind of lesson which I call point of view stories. Now the point of view story is designed to teach you grammar intuitively. There are a few things you need to know and think about when you’re doing this lesson.

The number one thing you should not think about are grammar rules. Do not think about grammar rules. Very important. I know you’ve been taught grammar rules for many years so it’s hard to get that out of your brain. You learned grammar in an analytical way — you intellectually learned it, you memorized rules, you took tests.

But you didn’t learn it intuitively, naturally. It did not get deep into your brain the way a native speaker knows grammar. So the point of view stories are designed to teach you the way a native speaker learns grammar. And we learn grammar by listening, by understanding meaning, by understanding patterns.

So when you listen to these stories, just listen. Smile. Shoulders back. Feel great and just listen. Don’t think about rules. Just relax and enjoy the story. Notice the patterns. In each story I’m going to tell this exact same story from the mini-story — the same story about Jan being rude — but from a different point of view. It means from a different time.

For example, I might tell that story but from the future. I’ll change some of the vocabulary, especially the verbs. All you have to do is just listen, notice some of the words change. I’ll be using will, for example. But forget this idea of the future tense. Forget your grammar books. Just notice the changes in vocabulary.

Now in the point of view stories I’m going to use two basic points of view. One is the future. The other I call the perfect tenses. All you need to know is I’ll start most of those stories with something like “Since she was young… Since 2001…” It means something started in the past and is continuing up until now.
Alright, enough talking, let’s get to it. Are you ready? Let’s start with the first one.

Since she was a child Jan has always been very rude. Since she was a child Jan has been rude. How long has Jan been rude? She’s been rude since she was a child. Was she rude when she was a baby? No, no, no, not when she was a baby. Just since she was a child, maybe since she was five years old.

So since she was a child she has been very rude, very harsh. And who has been very rude and harsh since she was a child? Well, of course, Jan. Jan has been very rude since she was a child. From being a child, maybe five or six years old, until now, all that time she has been rude. Has she always been rude?

Not always, just since she was a child. Since she was a child she has always said rude comments to people. Has she always said rude zingers to people? Since she was a child she has always said rude zingers to friends, to family, to everyone.

But one day, she summoned all her friends. And she said “I will never be harsh to you again.” She was very friendly. She coughed up compliments to each one. She said “Oh, you’re very nice and you’re very intelligent.” She coughed them up. It was difficult in the beginning.

But then over the course of two years she was very, very kind. She was always kind during that time. And, of course, after two years everybody loved Jan. They gave her kisses. They gave her money. They gave her love. And how did she feel? She felt very fulfilled.

Okay, so this is the end of the first POV story. Now something happened in that story and you should notice it. In the beginning I was saying “Since she was a child she has been rude.” Something started in the past and then it continued, up until now. But then it changed — it became a sudden one-time event: she summoned her friends. Boom, it happened, it finished.
And, you know, all you grammar people already know, I started using the past tense. But don’t think about that. The point is that it changed. Just notice when it changes. That’s all you need to do.

Alright, our next POV story, we’re going to the future. I have this idea for a movie. It’s going to happen in ten years. In ten years there will be a woman. She’ll be very, very rude. This woman will always say rude comments to her friends. She’ll give zingers to her friends every day.

What will her name be? Her name will be Jan. And Jan will be very, very rude. Her friends will cry and cry. But one day she’s going to change. One day she’s going to summon all her friends to her house. She’ll cough up compliments to each one. She’ll say “Oh, you’re very nice and you’re very intelligent.”

This will be tough for her in the beginning. But she’ll continue to be nice and kind over the course of two more years. And at the end of those two years everyone will love Jan. They’re going to give her kisses. They’re going to give her love. She’ll feel very, very fulfilled.

Just a couple of things to notice in these future stories. One is this “ll” sound — “Jan’ll be very rude” means “Jan will be very rude.” In real conversation we often just add “ll” for the future. Listen for that “ll”, it’s a little difficult to hear. Another one is gonna — gonna means going to, it means future.
Alright, that is the end of the POV stories. Just listen, listen, listen. Relax. Notice what’s happening in the stories but don’t think about it too much. Alright, I will see you next time.
sixmin: Rob: Hello, I'm Rob. Welcome to 6 Minute English, where we get together to discuss an interesting topic along with six useful items of vocabulary to wrestle with.

Catherine: Hello, I'm Catherine. Today's topic is all about togetherness — because we're talking about going to the gym and why gym membership is becoming increasingly popular. So Rob, do you head off to your local gym after work?
Rob: No — I hate group exercise. I prefer to go for a run on my own. It's free, and I enjoy being outdoors. How about you, Catherine?
Catherine: I really like group exercise, Rob. I find it's very motivating.
Rob: When something is motivating it makes us want to do something. So what motivates you to go to the gym, Catherine?

Catherine: Well, I go because I really like the spin classes. If I wasn't in a class, I don't think I would push myself so hard. I need someone to shout at me — to make me go faster and get up those hills.

Rob: If you push yourself, you force yourself to try harder — you set yourself a challenge. You are part of a growing trend of people who like to work out. So, how many people are members of a gym here in the UK? Is it a) 1 in 2, b) 1 in 7, or c) 1 in 10?
Catherine: I'm going to go for 1 in 10.
Rob: We'll find out later. Now let's listen to Philip Mills, who runs the sports fitness company Les Mills. He has a theory to explain why group exercise has become so popular.

INSERT — Philip Mills, CEO of Les Mills International: As people have become more sedentary they've realised that they need to move for their health. We work about 35% more hours than we used to in the 60s, and people are too busy to play sports. Society has become fragmented and a lot of the things that used to bring people together don't exist anymore. But we're tribal animals — pack animals — and the gym has been one of the things that helps people to commune.
Catherine: Yes, our sedentary lifestyles! We do all spend too much time sitting down, don't we, Rob?

Rob: According to Philip, we're probably spending more time sitting down than we used to because we're working a lot more. Society has become fragmented — and fragmented means broken up into small pieces.

Catherine: We miss that social connectedness — that feeling of belonging to a group — because we are essentially tribal animals. And a tribe is a group of people who live together and share the same language and culture.
Rob: Even now that most of us have left tribal society behind, we still want to feel part of a group that we can commune with — which means to get close to someone or something.

Rob: Remember I asked how many people are members of a gym in the UK? You said 1 in 10 — and you were wrong, I'm afraid! Figures show that one in seven of the UK population is a member of a gym. And more and more will be signing up — total membership could soon exceed ten million for the first time.

pairs.q: How was Jan?
pairs.a: Jan was rude. Jan was rude and harsh to everyone.

pairs.q: Who was very harsh and rude to everyone?
pairs.a: Jan, Jan was very harsh and was very rude to everyone.

pairs.q: Was Jan gentle and kind?
pairs.a: No. She was not gentle and kind. She was harsh. She was rude.

pairs.q: Was she rude and harsh to some people?
pairs.a: Not to some people, to everyone. She was rude and harsh to everyone.

pairs.q: Zinger? What’s a zinger?
pairs.a: A zinger is something that’s surprising or shocking. So a zinger of a comment means a surprise comment. Jan always said rude zingers. It means rude, surprising comments.

pairs.q: Were her zingers rude or funny?
pairs.a: Her zingers were rude. Her surprising comments were always rude.

pairs.q: Were her zingers kind?
pairs.a: No, not kind. Her zingers, her surprising comments were always harsh and rude.

pairs.q: Who always gave harsh zingers to her friends?
pairs.a: Jan, Jan always gave, Jan always said harsh zingers to her friends.

pairs.q: What kind of zingers?
pairs.a: Harsh zingers, rude zingers.

pairs.q: How often?
pairs.a: All the time.

pairs.q: Were her friends happy?
pairs.a: No, they cried.

pairs.q: What did she do?
pairs.a: She summoned her friends to her house.

pairs.q: Did she call her friends to her house?
pairs.a: Yes, she did. She called them to her house.

pairs.q: Who did she summon?
pairs.a: Her friends, Jan summoned her friends. She called her friends “Come to my house.” She summoned them.

pairs.q: Which friends did she summon?
pairs.a: Well, all of them. She summoned every friend. Jan summoned all of her friends to her house.

pairs.q: Did the compliments come out easily?
pairs.a: Oh, no, they didn’t. They didn’t come out easily. She had to force them out. She had to cough them up.

pairs.q: What did Jan cough up?
pairs.a: Compliments, a compliment is a nice statement. Something you say that’s nice to someone. She had to cough up compliments.

pairs.q: Who had to cough up compliments?
pairs.a: Jan, Jan coughed up compliments to her friends.

pairs.q: Was she going to be harsh to her friends again?
pairs.a: No, she said “I will never be harsh to you again.”

pairs.q: How was she going to be?
pairs.a: She was going to be gentle and kind to all her friends. She was not going to be harsh ever again.

pairs.q: Over the course of how many years did she do this?
pairs.a: Well, over the course of two years, for two years, during two years. Over the course of two years.

pairs.q: Who was nice and kind over the course of two years?
pairs.a: Jan, Jan was always nice and kind and friendly over the course of two years.

pairs.q: For how long?
pairs.a: For two years, over the course of two years.

pairs.q: Over the course of two years, what did Jan do?
pairs.a: Well, Jan became very kind. Jan gave compliments every day, all the time, over the course of two years.

pairs.q: After two years, how did Jan feel?
pairs.a: She felt fulfilled. She felt satisfied and very, very happy.

pairs.q: Why did she feel fulfilled after two years?
pairs.a: Well, because now everybody loved her. After two years everybody loved Jan. So she felt fulfilled.

pairs.q: Who felt fulfilled?
pairs.a: Jan, Jan felt fulfilled. She felt loved. She felt appreciated. She felt fulfilled.

vocab.example: Kaizen means small improvements every day.
She always said rude zingers to her friends.

vocab.defEn: to call someone to come to you
vocab.example: Jan summoned all her friends to her house.

vocab.defEn: to force something out of yourself with difficulty
vocab.example: It wasn't easy, but she coughed up a compliment.

vocab.defEn: very rough or too strong; the opposite of gentle
vocab.example: Don't use a harsh tone with yourself.
She decided to be gentle and kind.

vocab.defEn: to block or stop something from working
vocab.example: Fear will clog the creative process.

vocab.uz: oʻzini toʻla qoniqqan, mamnun his qilmoq
vocab.example: After two years, Jan felt fulfilled.

vocab.defEn: during, or across a period of time
vocab.example: She became kinder over the course of two years.

vocab.defEn: a nice thing you say about someone
vocab.example: He gave me a nice compliment.

vocab.defEn: to focus on or deal with something
vocab.example: Your brain will address the question.

warmup.uz: Suhbatda bir kishi Yangi yil vaʼdasini aytadi: “parhezga oʻtaman” deydi. Kayzen esa katta vaʼda emas, kichik kunlik qadam muhim deb oʻrgatadi. Sizningcha, u vaʼdasida turadimi?
warmup.en: In this dialogue someone shares a New Year's resolution — a big promise to change his diet. Kaizen says tiny daily steps beat big promises. Do you think he'll stick to it?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.en: So, did I tell you about my New Year's resolution? I've decided to go on a diet.

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.en: And you're going to completely transform your eating habits, right?

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.en: Exactly! I'm going to cut out all that junk I eat; no more chips, no more soda, no more fried food.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.en: But this time I'm going to stick to it. I really mean it! Trust me, Carol, I'm going to be a new man in one year's time!

dialogue.speaker: B
dialogue.en: Well, I guess we'll just have to wait and see.

dialogue.speaker: A
dialogue.en: Thanks, honey, that was a great meal. I'm stuffed. Do we have any chips left?

keyVocab.uz: Yangi yil vaʼdasi (yangi yilda biror ishni qilishga qatʼiy niyat)
keyVocab.defEn: a firm decision to do something in the new year
to start an eating plan to lose weight
the usual ways in which you eat

keyVocab.uz: vaʼdada turmoq, tashlab qoʻymay davom ettirmoq
keyVocab.defEn: to continue doing something you decided to do
to wait patiently to find out what will happen

quiz.q: How many people are members of a gym here in the UK?
quiz.qUz: Buyuk Britaniyada qancha odam sport zaliga aʼzo?
quiz.explanationUz: Toʻgʻri javob: b) 1 in 7 — har 7 kishidan 1 tasi sport zaliga aʼzo. Catherine 1 in 10 dedi, lekin haqiqiy raqam undan koʻproq, va u tobora oshib bormoqda (better and better — grammatika B!).

vocab.defEn: making you want to do something

vocab.uz: oʻzingizni qiynab, zoʻr berib harakat qilmoq
vocab.defEn: to force yourself to try harder
to exercise in order to improve your health and fitness

vocab.uz: qabilaga oid (qabila — birga yashab, bir til va madaniyatni baham koʻradigan guruh)
vocab.defEn: relating to a tribe: a group of people who live together and share a language and culture
to get close to someone or something

funEnglish.title: The Kaizen Mindset: How to Master Change with Small Improvements

speakingPrompt.en: Have you ever tried to change a habit? How long have you been working on it — and how has it changed you, little by little?
speakingPrompt.uz: Taxminan 60 soniya gapiring. Bu haftaning ikki grammatikasidan foydalaning: present perfect (I have … since/for …; How long have you …?) va bosqichma-bosqich oʻzgarish (better and better; get + qiyosiy daraja).
